title: Conservation of mass — why atoms never disappear
examTip: If a question asks why mass changed in an open reaction, your answer must mention a gas entering or leaving the system. Atoms are never created or destroyed — only rearranged.
introTips: Practise balancing equations by counting atoms on each side — it's a skill that gets faster with repetition.
Remember: you can only change the NUMBERS in front of formulae, never the formulae themselves.
Watch out for gases — reactions in open containers can gain or lose mass because of gas exchange with the air.
intro: Every time something burns, rusts, or explodes, you might think matter is being created or destroyed. But in 1774, Antoine Lavoisier proved something profound: atoms never vanish — they just rearrange. This idea, the law of conservation of mass, underpins every equation you'll ever write in chemistry. Get this right and you can predict exactly what happens in any reaction — and spot immediately if an equation is wrong.

keyPoints.heading: The law of conservation of mass
keyPoints.content: In any chemical reaction, the total mass of reactants = total mass of products. Atoms are not created or destroyed — they are rearranged into new substances. This is why equations must have the same number of each type of atom on both sides. Antoine Lavoisier demonstrated this in 1774 using carefully sealed vessels: no matter what reaction happened inside, the total mass before and after was always the same. Real-world examples: magnesium burns and the ash (MgO) is heavier than the original magnesium, because oxygen from the air has been incorporated (mass of Mg + mass of O₂ = mass of MgO). Dissolving sugar in water: the total mass of the sugar + water is identical to the mass of the sugar solution.
writeBullets: Conservation of mass — total mass of reactants always equals total mass of products
Atoms are rearranged in chemical reactions, not created or destroyed
Lavoisier (1774) — first proved conservation of mass using sealed vessels
Magnesium burning gains mass because it combines with oxygen from the air
Equations must have equal numbers of each atom on both sides — this is why we balance them

keyTerms.def: The total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products in any chemical reaction
French chemist (1774) who proved conservation of mass through careful experiments with sealed vessels

keyPoints.memTip: Atoms are like Lego bricks — you rearrange them into new shapes, but the total number of bricks never changes.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Emphasise that conservation applies to the total system — apparent gains or losses are always due to gases entering or leaving an open system.

keyPoints.heading: Balancing chemical equations
keyPoints.content:
A chemical equation must have the same number of each type of atom on both sides. To balance an equation: write the word equation → substitute correct symbols and formulae → count atoms on each side → add coefficients (numbers in front of formulae) to balance → never change the formulae themselves.

Example 1: H₂ + O₂ → H₂O — oxygen is unbalanced (2 on left, 1 on right). Balanced: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O ✓

Example 2: Mg + HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂ — Cl and H are unbalanced. Balanced: Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂ ✓

Example 3: Methane combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O ✓

Check: count every atom on both sides. If totals match, the equation is balanced.
writeBullets: Balanced equation — same number of each type of atom on both sides of the arrow
Coefficients — the numbers placed in front of formulae to balance equations
Never change formulae (subscripts) to balance — only add or change coefficients
2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O: 4 H atoms and 2 O atoms on each side ✓
Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂: 1 Mg, 2 Cl, 2 H on each side ✓
Always check by counting every atom type on both sides after balancing

keyTerms.def: The number written in front of a formula in an equation to show how many moles are involved
An equation where the number of each type of atom is equal on both sides

keyPoints.memTip: Balancing = adjusting the multipliers, never the formulae. Like scaling a recipe — you change how many batches, not the ingredients.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students frequently change subscripts (e.g., writing H₃ instead of H₂) to 'balance' — make clear this creates a different (wrong) substance.

keyPoints.heading: Why mass appears to change in open systems
keyPoints.content: In a sealed system, mass is always conserved. But in an open system, gases can enter or leave, so the measured mass changes — even though atoms are still conserved overall. Mass decreases if a gas is produced and escapes. Example: CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂. If done in an open beaker, the CO₂ gas escapes into the air — the mass of the beaker and contents decreases. The 'lost' mass has left as CO₂ gas. Mass increases if gas is absorbed from the air. Example: magnesium burning combines with oxygen from the air. The MgO product is heavier than the original Mg ribbon. In the exam, if asked 'why did the mass decrease/increase?', your answer must say: a gas was released and escaped / a gas from the air was absorbed.
writeBullets: Open system — gases can escape into or enter from the atmosphere, changing measured mass
Mass decreases — a gas product (e.g., CO₂) escapes the container into the air
Mass increases — a gas from air (e.g., O₂) is absorbed and incorporated into the product
Total mass is still conserved — but some is no longer in the container being measured
Sealed system — all mass stays contained, so measured mass is always conserved

keyTerms.def: A reaction system where matter (especially gases) can enter or leave the container
A reaction system where no matter can enter or leave; mass measured is always conserved

keyPoints.memTip: If mass changes, ask: was a gas made and released? Or was a gas absorbed from the air? One of those two is always the answer.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This is one of AQA's favourite 2-mark explanation questions. Students must specifically name the gas involved, not just say 'gas escaped'.

keyPoints.heading: Calculations from balanced equations — finding masses of reactants and products
keyPoints.content:
Balanced equations let you calculate exact masses of reactants and products. Use molar masses and the mole ratio method.

Worked example: What mass of water is produced when 4 g of hydrogen reacts completely with oxygen?
Balanced equation: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
Mr of H₂ = 2 g/mol. Mr of H₂O = 18 g/mol.
Moles of H₂ = 4 ÷ 2 = 2 mol.
Ratio H₂ : H₂O = 2 : 2 = 1 : 1, so moles of H₂O = 2 mol.
Mass of H₂O = 2 × 18 = 36 g.

Check using conservation of mass: Mass of O₂ used = total products − H₂ used = 36 − 4 = 32 g. Moles of O₂ = 32 ÷ 32 = 1 mol. Ratio 2H₂ : 1O₂ → 2 mol H₂ : 1 mol O₂ ✓
writeBullets: Step 1: write the balanced equation and identify molar masses
Step 2: calculate moles of known substance using n = m ÷ Mr
Step 3: use the mole ratio from the equation to find moles of unknown
Step 4: convert moles to mass using m = n × Mr
Conservation check: total mass of products should equal total mass of reactants
Example: 4 g H₂ + 32 g O₂ → 36 g H₂O (4 + 32 = 36 ✓)

keyTerms.def: The ratio of moles of substances in a reaction, given by coefficients in the balanced equation
The mass of one mole of a substance in g/mol; equal to Mr numerically

keyPoints.memTip: Always check your answer with conservation of mass — reactant masses should add up to product masses.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Encourage students to use conservation of mass as a checking tool at the end of every calculation — it catches arithmetic errors.

tables.title: Balancing equations — practice examples
rows: Added coefficient 2 in front of H₂ and H₂O
Added coefficient 2 in front of HCl
Added coefficient 2 in front of O₂ and H₂O
Added coefficients 2 in front of Na, H₂O, and NaOH

1.question: Balance this equation: Mg + HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂. What coefficient goes in front of HCl?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂. You need 2 HCl to supply 2 Cl atoms for MgCl₂.
1.wrongFeedback: Count the chlorine atoms: MgCl₂ needs 2 Cl on the right side, so you need 2HCl on the left. The answer is 2.

2.question: Calcium carbonate reacts with hydrochloric acid in an open beaker, producing CO₂ gas. What happens to the measured mass?
options: It decreases because CO₂ gas escapes into the air
It stays the same because mass is always conserved
It increases because the acid adds mass
It decreases because atoms are destroyed in the reaction
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right! CO₂ is a gaseous product that escapes the open beaker, so the measured mass decreases — even though total mass is conserved.
2.wrongFeedback: The measured mass decreases because CO₂ gas is produced and escapes into the air. Mass IS conserved overall, but some of that mass is now in the atmosphere as CO₂.

3.question: In the reaction 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, what mass of water is produced from 6 g of H₂? (Mr H₂ = 2, Mr H₂O = 18)
3.correctFeedback: Brilliant! n(H₂) = 6 ÷ 2 = 3 mol. Ratio 2:2 = 1:1, so n(H₂O) = 3 mol. m = 3 × 18 = 54 g.
3.wrongFeedback: Work through the steps: n(H₂) = 6 ÷ 2 = 3 mol. Ratio H₂:H₂O = 2:2 = 1:1, so 3 mol H₂O. m = 3 × 18 = 54 g.

commonMistakes: Changing the subscripts in a formula (e.g., writing H₃ or O₃) to try to balance an equation — you must only change the coefficients in front of formulae.
Saying 'mass was lost' when asked to explain a decrease — you must say a gaseous product escaped into the atmosphere.
Forgetting that when a metal burns in air, mass INCREASES because oxygen from the air is incorporated into the product.
Not checking the equation is balanced before using it for calculations — an unbalanced equation gives the wrong mole ratio.
Assuming conservation of mass means the measured mass always stays constant — it only stays constant in a sealed system; gases entering or leaving an open system change the measured mass.
examTips: For any 'explain why the mass changed' question, always name the specific gas and say whether it was produced (escaped) or absorbed from air.
When balancing equations, work element by element. Leave oxygen until last if it appears on its own (O₂) — it's easiest to adjust.
Use conservation of mass to check your calculation answers: the total mass of products must equal the total mass of reactants.
In calculations, always state the balanced equation first — you'll get a method mark even if you make an arithmetic error later.
revisionCardBullets: Conservation of mass: total mass of reactants = total mass of products
Atoms are rearranged — never created or destroyed — in chemical reactions
Lavoisier (1774) proved conservation using sealed vessels
Balanced equation: same number of each atom on both sides
Only change COEFFICIENTS to balance — never change formulae
Open system: gas escapes → measured mass decreases
Open system: gas absorbed from air → measured mass increases
Mass IS conserved — but gases not in the container aren't measured
Magnesium burning: gains mass because it absorbs O₂ from air
CaCO₃ + HCl in open beaker: mass falls as CO₂ escapes
Use mole ratio from balanced equation to calculate masses
Conservation of mass = your checking tool after every calculation
